passages.title: Humanities: The Unfinished Ruin
passages.passage_text:
We tend to assume that a work of art is finished when its maker declares it so, or when death forecloses further revision. Yet the fragment resists this tidy logic. Consider the appeal of the architectural ruin, which no architect ever intended. A crumbling colonnade, half-swallowed by ivy, moves us in ways the intact temple rarely does. The conventional explanation is nostalgia: we mourn a lost grandeur and project onto the stones a vanished wholeness. But this explanation, I suspect, gets the experience backward. The ruin does not merely gesture toward a complete original that once stood; it offers something the complete building could not. In its broken state it invites the imagination to participate, to supply the missing arch, to guess at the roofline. The viewer becomes, briefly, a collaborator.

This is why deliberate fragments—the unfinished sculpture, the abandoned poem—can carry an authority that polished works forfeit. When Michelangelo left figures straining half-emerged from raw marble, he did not fail to finish them. Or rather, whether he finished them is beside the point, for the incompleteness itself performs an idea: the struggle of form to escape its material. A smoothly resolved statue would state that struggle; the fragment enacts it. Here the gap between intention and accident dissolves. It hardly matters whether the artist planned the incompleteness, because the beholder's response does not depend on knowing.

Some will object that this celebrates mere damage, that it flatters our tendency to read meaning into rubble. And there is a real danger here. Not every broken thing is eloquent; a shattered vase is usually just a shattered vase. The fragment speaks only when enough structure survives to imply the whole it withholds. Ruin becomes art at the precise threshold where absence remains legible as absence—where we can feel the shape of what is gone. Beyond that threshold lies noise; short of it lies mere completeness. The finished work, then, may be less an achievement than a foreclosure. It answers every question it raises and, in answering, sends us away. The fragment keeps us. It hands us the labor of imagining, and in that labor we are no longer spectators but makers.

questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the central thesis of the passage?

options.A: Ruins are valuable primarily because they preserve traces of a lost historical grandeur.
options.B: Incompleteness can give a work an appeal that a finished work lacks by prompting the viewer to imaginatively supply what is missing.
options.C: Artists such as Michelangelo deliberately left works unfinished in order to demonstrate technical humility.
options.D: Any broken object becomes art once a viewer decides to find meaning in it.

questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly argues that the fragment 'invites the imagination to participate' and 'hands us the labor of imagining,' offering something the complete work 'could not.' This captures both the value claimed for incompleteness and the mechanism (the viewer's participation), which is the author's core point.

distractor_notes.A: This is the 'nostalgia' explanation the author explicitly rejects as getting 'the experience backward.'
distractor_notes.C: The author says whether Michelangelo intended incompleteness is 'beside the point'; intention is not the thesis, and 'technical humility' is never claimed.
distractor_notes.D: This is too extreme and directly contradicted: 'a shattered vase is usually just a shattered vase,' and the fragment 'speaks only when enough structure survives.'

options.A: concede that completed works are, on balance, more satisfying than fragments.
options.B: explain why viewers tend to forget the details of finished works.
options.C: contrast the passivity a finished work produces with the active engagement a fragment invites.
options.D: prove that all questions raised by art can be definitively answered.

questions.explanation: The sentence is immediately set against 'The fragment keeps us,' which 'hands us the labor of imagining.' Its function is to draw the contrast between the finished work's closure (which dismisses the viewer) and the fragment's ongoing engagement, supporting the author's preference for participatory experience.

distractor_notes.A: This reverses the author's evaluation; the finished work is called a 'foreclosure,' not more satisfying.
distractor_notes.B: Memory of details is never discussed; this is out of scope.
distractor_notes.D: The claim is not offered as proof about art in general; it is a rhetorical characterization of finished works specifically, used for contrast.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, the phrase 'legible as absence' most nearly means:

options.A: physically restored so the original form can be read.
options.B: recognizable as a meaningful gap whose missing whole can still be sensed.
options.C: documented in written records that describe the lost structure.
options.D: so damaged that no original form can be inferred.

questions.explanation: The phrase appears where 'we can feel the shape of what is gone' and 'enough structure survives to imply the whole it withholds.' Absence is 'legible' when the viewer can still read the missing whole—recognize the gap as meaningful.

distractor_notes.A: 'Legible as absence' concerns perceiving what is missing, not restoring or filling it in physically.
distractor_notes.C: Written documentation is never mentioned; the reading is done by the imagination confronting the object itself.
distractor_notes.D: This describes the state 'beyond that threshold' that the author calls 'noise'—the opposite of legible absence.

options.B: wholly persuaded, ultimately abandoning the defense of the fragment.
options.C: partly sympathetic, granting the danger while maintaining that a real threshold distinguishes eloquent fragments from mere debris.

distractor_notes.B: He does not abandon the defense; he restates it with the threshold qualification.

questions.stem: Suppose researchers discovered a poem that its author had clearly and carefully completed, yet which readers overwhelmingly experience as tantalizingly open-ended and imaginatively demanding. How would this discovery bear on the author's argument?

distractor_notes.C: The passage explicitly invokes 'the abandoned poem,' so poetry is within scope.

passages.title: Social Sciences: Trust and the Marketplace
passages.passage_text:
Economists once liked to imagine the market as a machine that runs on self-interest alone. Buyers and sellers, each pursuing advantage, would coordinate through prices without needing to like or even trust one another. On this view, trust is a pleasant residue of commerce, not a precondition for it. Yet the more closely one examines how markets actually function, the harder this austere picture is to sustain. Every transaction that unfolds over time—a loan repaid next year, goods shipped before payment clears, a service promised for later delivery—requires one party to rely on another's future conduct. Contracts and courts can enforce some of this reliance, but enforcement is costly and slow, and no society could afford to litigate every exchange. What fills the gap is trust: the willingness to proceed without a guarantee.

This observation might seem to reverse the older picture, making trust the true engine of exchange. But that overcorrection carries its own error. Trust is not a substitute for self-interest; it is often produced by it. A merchant who cheats a customer may gain once, but a merchant known to deal honestly attracts repeat business, referrals, and credit. Reputation, in other words, turns honesty into a self-interested strategy. The trader is trustworthy not despite caring about profit but because he does. Where relationships are ongoing and reputations circulate, the pursuit of advantage can quietly manufacture the very reliability that pure self-interest was supposed to make unnecessary.

The implications are uncomfortable for both camps. Against the cynic who insists that commerce is merely disguised greed, we can say that markets breed cooperative habits, norms of fair dealing that participants come to value in themselves. Against the romantic who imagines that trade rests on some noble fellow-feeling, we can note that these habits flourish precisely where they pay. The interesting cases lie between: the anonymous one-time transaction, where reputation cannot form and neither party will meet again. Here trust has the thinnest soil, and here—tellingly—we see the most elaborate scaffolding of law, licensing, and third-party verification. Societies build formal institutions exactly where informal trust is weakest. If trust were merely a residue, we would not spend so much to replace it when it fails to appear.

questions.stem: The main point of the passage is that:

options.A: trust is an irrelevant sentiment that efficient markets can discard entirely.
options.B: markets rest on trust, which is best understood as a noble fellow-feeling opposed to self-interest.
options.C: trust is essential to exchange, but it is frequently generated by self-interest rather than opposed to it.
options.D: formal legal institutions are always superior to informal trust in governing exchange.

questions.explanation: The passage argues both that trust is a precondition for exchange ('the willingness to proceed without a guarantee') and that reputation 'turns honesty into a self-interested strategy'—self-interest can 'manufacture the very reliability' it seemed to make unnecessary. The synthesis of these two claims is the main point.

distractor_notes.A: This is the 'austere picture' the author says is 'hard to sustain,' not the conclusion.
distractor_notes.B: The author explicitly rejects the 'romantic' view that trade rests on 'noble fellow-feeling.'
distractor_notes.D: The author notes formal institutions arise where trust is weak, but never claims they are 'always superior'—that overstates his position.

questions.stem: The author mentions that societies build 'the most elaborate scaffolding of law, licensing, and third-party verification' around anonymous one-time transactions primarily in order to:

options.A: recommend that all transactions be governed by formal law rather than trust.
options.B: provide evidence that trust is important, since its absence prompts costly institutional substitutes.
options.C: demonstrate that reputation forms most easily among strangers.
options.D: argue that anonymous transactions are more common than repeated ones.

questions.explanation: The example directly supports the closing inference: 'If trust were merely a residue, we would not spend so much to replace it when it fails to appear.' The elaborate scaffolding appears where informal trust is weakest, which demonstrates trust's importance by showing what its absence costs.

distractor_notes.A: The author observes this pattern but does not recommend replacing all trust with law; he treats formal institutions as a costly gap-filler.
distractor_notes.C: This reverses the passage: among strangers 'reputation cannot form' and 'trust has the thinnest soil.'
distractor_notes.D: Relative frequency of transaction types is never claimed.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, the description of trust as a 'residue' most nearly conveys the idea that trust is:

options.A: a byproduct that appears alongside commerce but is not required to make it work.
options.B: a toxic remnant that damages market efficiency.
options.C: the fundamental cause that makes all commerce possible.
options.D: a legal instrument formally enforced by courts.

questions.explanation: The 'residue' language belongs to the older view that trust is 'a pleasant residue of commerce, not a precondition for it.' A residue is something left over—present but non-essential—which is precisely the position the author sets out to challenge.

distractor_notes.B: 'Residue' here means incidental leftover, not something harmful; nothing suggests trust damages markets.
distractor_notes.C: This is the opposite of 'residue'; the author later argues trust IS important, but the word 'residue' names the view that it is NOT foundational.
distractor_notes.D: The passage contrasts trust with formal enforcement by courts; a residue is informal, not a legal instrument.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the author's claim about reputation depends?

options.A: That merchants are incapable of ever behaving honestly.
options.B: That information about a trader's past conduct can circulate to future potential partners.
options.C: That courts are entirely unable to enforce any contract.
options.D: That every transaction in a market is anonymous and one-time.

questions.explanation: The argument that honesty becomes self-interested rests on 'reputations circulate'—an honest merchant 'attracts repeat business, referrals, and credit.' This mechanism only works if information about past conduct reaches future partners, which the author assumes.

distractor_notes.A: The author's point is the reverse—reputation makes honesty attractive; he does not assume merchants cannot be honest.
distractor_notes.C: The author says enforcement is 'costly and slow,' not that courts can enforce nothing; his reputation argument does not require total court failure.
distractor_notes.D: The reputation mechanism assumes the opposite—'relationships are ongoing'; anonymous one-time deals are the case where it fails.

questions.stem: The author's overall tone toward both the 'cynic' and the 'romantic' can best be characterized as:

options.A: enthusiastic endorsement of the romantic and rejection of the cynic.
options.B: even-handedly critical, faulting each for capturing only part of the truth.
options.C: neutral and purely descriptive, expressing no evaluation of either.
options.D: mocking contempt directed equally at both.

questions.explanation: The passage says the implications are 'uncomfortable for both camps,' then corrects the cynic ('markets breed cooperative habits...valued in themselves') and the romantic ('these habits flourish precisely where they pay'). The author critiques each symmetrically for a partial view.

distractor_notes.A: He does not endorse the romantic; he corrects the romantic just as he corrects the cynic.
distractor_notes.C: The tone is evaluative, not neutral—he explicitly says each side errs.
distractor_notes.D: The tone is corrective and analytical, not contemptuous or mocking.

questions.stem: A new online platform allows one-time buyers and sellers who will never interact again to leave permanent public ratings visible to all future users. According to the passage's reasoning, this feature would most likely:

options.A: have no effect, because the passage holds that trust cannot influence anonymous transactions under any circumstances.
options.B: extend reputational incentives to otherwise anonymous exchanges, enabling self-interest to foster trustworthy behavior even among strangers.
options.C: eliminate the need for any honest behavior, since ratings replace character.
options.D: confirm the romantic view that trade rests on noble fellow-feeling.

questions.explanation: The passage says reputation makes honesty self-interested 'where relationships are ongoing and reputations circulate.' A permanent public rating visible to future users lets reputation circulate even among one-time strangers, so the platform would extend the reputational mechanism to previously reputation-free transactions—exactly the author's logic applied to a new case.

distractor_notes.A: The passage attributes weak trust in anonymous deals to the fact that reputation 'cannot form'; a system that lets it form removes that obstacle, so trust CAN be influenced.
distractor_notes.C: The mechanism works by making honesty pay, not by removing the need for honest conduct; ratings incentivize honesty rather than replace it.
distractor_notes.D: The scenario operates through self-interest and reputation, which the author distinguishes from the romantic's 'noble fellow-feeling.'

passages.title: Humanities: Translation as Betrayal
passages.passage_text:
There is an old Italian pun that translators are traitors—traduttore, traditore. The charge is usually understood as a lament: something precious is lost when a poem crosses from one language into another, and the translator, however skilled, presides over the loss. Sound, rhythm, the peculiar weight a word carries in its native soil—these evaporate, and what remains is a serviceable ghost. I do not deny the loss. But I want to question whether it is the whole story, or even the most interesting part of it.

Consider that the original poem, too, was once a kind of translation. The poet did not invent language; she inherited it, with all its accidents and associations, and bent it toward a meaning that did not yet exist. Every genuine poem strains against the ready-made phrases it is built from. In this sense the poet is already a translator, carrying private experience into public words, and losing something in that crossing as well. If we grant that the original is not a pristine origin but itself a negotiation, then the translator's labor looks less like a fall from grace than a continuation of the same work by other means.

This reframing does not excuse bad translation, and it would be sentimental to pretend that all losses are secretly gains. Some are simply losses. Yet the best translations are not those that minimize damage; they are those that discover, in the target language, resources the original could never have used. A pun impossible in the source tongue, a rhythm native only to the new language—these are not compensations for what is lost but additions the original could not contain. The translated poem becomes, at its finest, a second original: bound to the first, yet answerable to it rather than merely derived from it. To call the translator a traitor, then, is not entirely wrong. But treason implies a settled loyalty betrayed, and here the loyalty is divided from the start—owed at once to the author's meaning and to the new language's genius. The translator serves two masters, and the accusation of betrayal mistakes that double service for a simple failure to be faithful.

questions.stem: The central thesis of the passage is that translation is best understood as:

options.A: an inevitable failure in which the translator betrays the original author's intentions.
options.B: a continuation of the creative negotiation begun by the poet, capable of producing a work with its own value rather than a mere degraded copy.
options.C: a purely mechanical process that neither adds nor loses anything of importance.
options.D: a superior art form that consistently improves upon the original poems.

questions.explanation: The author argues the poet is 'already a translator' and that translation is 'a continuation of the same work by other means,' with the best translations becoming 'a second original.' The thesis reframes translation as creative negotiation yielding independent value, not simple loss.

distractor_notes.A: This is the traditional 'lament' the author sets out to complicate, not his conclusion.
distractor_notes.C: The author stresses that translation involves both real loss and genuine addition; it is far from mechanical or neutral.
distractor_notes.D: The author says it is 'sentimental to pretend that all losses are secretly gains'; he does not claim translation consistently improves originals.

questions.stem: The author's assertion that 'the original poem, too, was once a kind of translation' functions in the argument primarily to:

options.A: prove that no poem can ever be truly original.
options.B: concede that translators inevitably distort the works they handle.
options.C: undermine the assumption that the original is a pristine origin, thereby recasting the translator's work as a continuation rather than a fall.
options.D: argue that all languages are ultimately identical in their resources.

questions.explanation: Right after this claim the author writes that if the original 'is not a pristine origin but itself a negotiation,' then translation 'looks less like a fall from grace than a continuation.' The claim's function is to dissolve the special status of the original so translation can be reframed as the same kind of work.

distractor_notes.A: The author is redefining originality (the poet 'bent' language 'toward a meaning that did not yet exist'), not proving originality impossible.
distractor_notes.B: This is a claim about translators; the sentence is instead about the original poem, and its purpose is to elevate translation, not to concede distortion.
distractor_notes.D: The passage actually emphasizes that languages differ—each has its own 'genius' and resources—so it does not treat languages as identical.

questions.stem: As used in the passage, the phrase 'a serviceable ghost' most nearly refers to a translation that is:

options.A: usable yet drained of the vivid qualities the original possessed.
options.B: frightening to readers who prefer the source language.
options.C: more vivid and alive than the poem it renders.
options.D: unusable and entirely without value.

questions.explanation: The phrase completes the lament that 'sound, rhythm, the peculiar weight a word carries...evaporate, and what remains is a serviceable ghost.' 'Serviceable' means still usable, while 'ghost' conveys that the living qualities have departed—usable but drained.

distractor_notes.B: 'Ghost' is metaphorical for lost vitality, not literal fright; no reader reaction is described.
distractor_notes.C: This reverses the sense; the ghost is what remains after vividness has 'evaporated,' so it is less alive, not more.
distractor_notes.D: 'Serviceable' explicitly means usable, so the phrase does not mean without value.

options.C: Qualified acceptance, allowing that the saying is 'not entirely wrong' while arguing it misdescribes a divided loyalty as a simple failure.

distractor_notes.B: He does not totally reject it; he grants it is 'not entirely wrong.'

options.A: reproduces the sound and rhythm of the original as closely as the new language permits.
options.B: exploits resources unique to the target language to create effects the original could not have contained.
options.C: adds nothing and subtracts nothing from the source poem.
options.D: abandons any obligation to the original author's meaning.

questions.explanation: The author states directly that 'the best translations are not those that minimize damage; they are those that discover, in the target language, resources the original could never have used'—'additions the original could not contain.' Success is defined by creative use of the new language's distinctive resources.

distractor_notes.A: This is a reasonable-sounding goal, but the author explicitly says the best translations are NOT those that merely minimize damage or preserve the original's features.
distractor_notes.C: The author's ideal translation actively adds resources; a neutral transfer that changes nothing is not his standard.
distractor_notes.D: The author insists loyalty is 'owed at once to the author's meaning and to the new language'; abandoning the author's meaning breaks that double service.

questions.stem: A critic argues that a translated novel is worthless unless a bilingual reader, comparing it line by line with the original, finds no meaning altered. This critic's standard is most directly at odds with the passage's claim that:

options.B: the finest translations add resources the original could not contain and become a kind of second original.
options.C: the original poet inherited language rather than inventing it.
options.D: sound and rhythm often evaporate when a poem crosses languages.

This clashes most directly with the author's view that the best translations deliberately add 'resources the original could never have used' and become 'a second original' answerable to the first rather than merely derived from it—alteration and addition are, for the author, sources of value.

distractor_notes.C: The origin of the poet's language is unrelated to the critic's line-by-line fidelity standard.
distractor_notes.D: The author agrees that sound and rhythm are lost, so this shared observation is not where the critic conflicts with him.

passages.title: Social Sciences: The Myth of Neutral Maps
passages.passage_text:
We tend to treat the map as a humble servant of fact, a faithful transcription of the world onto paper. Yet every map is an argument disguised as a description. To draw a map is to decide what counts as worth showing and what may be discarded as noise, and no such decision is innocent. The cartographer who enlarges a capital city and shrinks the villages around it is not lying about distances; he is instructing us, quietly, about where importance is thought to reside.

Consider the familiar projection that hangs in classrooms, which inflates the northern landmasses while compressing the equatorial ones. Generations of students have absorbed, without a word of argument, a picture in which certain continents loom and others recede. The mapmaker may protest that he merely obeyed the mathematics of flattening a sphere, and this is true. But the choice of which distortion to accept, among the many available, was never dictated by mathematics alone. It reflected which errors the makers were willing to tolerate, and whose sense of proportion the map was meant to flatter.

Some critics conclude from this that maps are simply propaganda, tools of whichever power commissions them. This overstates the case. A map that misrepresents a coastline will run ships aground regardless of the intentions behind it, and mariners are unforgiving editors. The discipline of use imposes a real constraint: a map must work, and working requires a certain fidelity to the terrain. The interesting truth lies between the naive and the cynical views. Maps are neither transparent windows nor pure fictions. They are selective instruments, answerable to the world in some respects and to their makers in others.

What follows is not that we should distrust maps, but that we should read them as we read any argument, attentive to emphasis and omission. The reader who notices that a subway map straightens curves and equalizes distances has not caught the designer in a deception; she has understood the map's purpose, which is not to preserve geography but to make a system legible. To ask of a map only whether it is accurate is to ask the wrong question. The better question is what the map was trying to help us do, and whether, for that purpose, its silences and exaggerations serve us or mislead us.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?

options.A: Maps deceive their users because cartographers deliberately conceal inconvenient facts about terrain.
options.B: Maps are selective instruments that answer partly to the world and partly to their makers' purposes, and should be read as arguments.
options.C: The mathematics of projecting a sphere onto paper makes all map distortion unavoidable and therefore harmless.
options.D: Because every map serves the power that commissions it, no map can be trusted as a guide to reality.

questions.explanation: The author repeatedly stakes out a middle position: maps are 'neither transparent windows nor pure fictions' but 'selective instruments, answerable to the world in some respects and to their makers in others,' and urges reading them 'as we read any argument.' Choice B restates this balance directly.

distractor_notes.A: Too extreme and misattributes intent; the author explicitly denies that the cartographer who emphasizes a capital is 'lying,' and treats selection as instruction rather than deliberate deception.
distractor_notes.C: Reverses the author's point; he grants the mathematics is 'true' but insists the choice among distortions 'was never dictated by mathematics alone,' so distortion is not merely mechanical or harmless.
distractor_notes.D: This is the 'cynical view' the author explicitly rejects as an overstatement, noting that the discipline of use imposes 'a real constraint.'

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

questions.stem: In the passage, the phrase describing mariners as 'unforgiving editors' most nearly conveys that:

options.A: sailors historically rejected any map they had not helped to draw themselves.
options.B: practical use punishes inaccurate maps, forcing them toward genuine fidelity to the terrain.
options.C: navigation requires maps to be redrawn constantly as coastlines physically change.
options.D: professional editors were once employed to check maps before they reached the public.

questions.explanation: The phrase appears in the claim that 'a map that misrepresents a coastline will run ships aground regardless of the intentions behind it.' Calling mariners 'unforgiving editors' figuratively means that real-world use ruthlessly exposes and corrects error, imposing 'a certain fidelity to the terrain'—exactly choice B.

distractor_notes.A: Out of scope; the passage says nothing about sailors participating in mapmaking or rejecting maps they did not draw.
distractor_notes.C: Distorts the point; the constraint comes from the danger of inaccuracy, not from coastlines physically changing over time.
distractor_notes.D: Takes 'editors' too literally; the word is a metaphor for the corrective pressure of use, not an actual profession described in the passage.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the classroom projection that inflates northern landmasses can best be described as:

options.A: critical awareness that treats the projection as revealing rather than simply condemning it.
options.B: indifference, since all projections distort and none is worse than another.
options.C: indignant condemnation of a tool designed to mislead schoolchildren.
options.D: admiration for the mathematical ingenuity that made the projection possible.

passages.title: Humanities: The Unfinished Statue
passages.passage_text:
There is a peculiar power in the unfinished work of art, and it is worth asking why. A block of marble in which a figure seems to be struggling free, its limbs half-emerged and its surface still rough with the chisel's marks, can move us more deeply than a polished statue whose every fold is resolved. We are tempted to explain this away as sentimentality, a fondness for the story of the artist at labor. But the appeal runs deeper than biography.

The finished statue presents itself as a fact. It says: here is the form, complete and closed, and there is nothing left for you to do but admire it. The unfinished work, by contrast, extends an invitation. It leaves the imagination a task. Where the marble is only suggested, the eye supplies the missing contour; where the surface is raw, the mind completes the gesture it seems about to make. The viewer is drawn into the act of making, becoming, for a moment, a collaborator rather than a spectator.

One might object that this is merely to praise incompleteness for its own sake, as if any half-done thing were superior to a whole one. That would be absurd. A botched work is not made profound by being abandoned. The unfinished pieces that hold us are those in which enough has been achieved to make the whole imaginable, so that the gaps are felt as withheld rather than as absent. Incompleteness moves us only when it is legible as potential, when the fragment implies a plenitude it has not yet reached.

This suggests something about the nature of aesthetic pleasure more broadly. We often assume that beauty lies in resolution, in the satisfying closure of a form that leaves no question open. Yet the unfinished statue hints that a rival pleasure exists, one rooted not in completion but in the mind's own activity. The work that resolves everything asks nothing of us; the work that withholds a little asks us to lean in. Perhaps the deepest response to art is never passive reception but a kind of participation, and the finished masterpiece earns our reverence precisely by disguising how much it still leaves for us to imagine.

options.B: Aesthetic pleasure can arise from a viewer's active participation in completing a work, not only from a work's resolution.
options.C: The appeal of the unfinished statue is best explained by sentimental interest in the artist's biography.
options.D: Beauty resides entirely in the satisfying closure of a form that leaves no question unanswered.

questions.explanation: The passage argues that alongside the familiar pleasure of resolution, there is 'a rival pleasure... rooted not in completion but in the mind's own activity,' and that the deepest response to art is 'a kind of participation.' Choice B captures this thesis about active engagement as a genuine source of aesthetic pleasure.

distractor_notes.A: Too extreme; the author explicitly denies that 'any half-done thing' is superior and rejects mere labor as the explanation ('the appeal runs deeper than biography').
distractor_notes.C: The author raises the biography explanation only to set it aside as insufficient ('the appeal runs deeper than biography').
distractor_notes.D: Reverses the author's point; he questions the assumption that beauty lies wholly in resolution and posits a rival pleasure.

passages.title: Social Sciences: Trust and the Handshake
passages.passage_text:
Economists once modeled the marketplace as a meeting of strangers who need know nothing of one another beyond the terms of the deal at hand. On this view, the price system does all the work: it condenses the wants and capacities of countless anonymous actors into a single number, so that trust becomes an unnecessary luxury. If the contract is enforceable, why should it matter whether the parties like or believe in one another? The elegance of the model lay precisely in how little it demanded of human character.

But the model has always sat uneasily with what merchants actually do. Long before a contract is signed, they extend credit on a word, share information they are under no obligation to share, and forgive lapses a strict reading of the terms would punish. Such behavior looks irrational until one notices that most valuable exchange is not a single transaction but the opening move in a relationship expected to continue. In a world of repeated dealings, a reputation for fair conduct is itself an asset, and the trader who guards it will decline profitable cheating that a one-shot logic would recommend.

This does not mean, however, that trust simply replaces contracts, as some enthusiasts of 'social capital' suggest. Trust and formal enforcement are not rivals but partners. Courts and written terms provide the floor beneath which conduct cannot fall; trust operates in the vast space above that floor, where the law is silent or too costly to invoke. Remove the legal floor entirely and trust grows brittle, for the tempted party knows that betrayal carries no ultimate penalty. The two institutions lean on each other.

What the older model missed, then, was not that markets require affection—they do not—but that they require something economists were slow to name: a fabric of expectations about how others will behave when no one is watching. This fabric is not free. It must be woven over time, through conduct that is costly precisely because it forgoes short-term advantage, and it can be torn far faster than it is made. To treat it as a mere frill, dispensable once prices are known, is to mistake the scaffolding of exchange for its ornament. The handshake, it turns out, was never a quaint survival from a pre-modern age. It was doing work the ledger could not.

options.D: Merchants behave irrationally when they extend credit or forgive lapses not required by their contracts.

questions.explanation: The author argues the older price-only model 'missed' the need for 'a fabric of expectations,' that this fabric is 'not free' and 'must be woven over time,' and that trust and enforcement 'are not rivals but partners.' Choice C combines these threads: costly-built trust operating alongside contracts.

distractor_notes.A: This is the older model the author critiques for missing what markets actually require.
distractor_notes.B: The author explicitly rejects the claim that 'trust simply replaces contracts,' insisting the two 'lean on each other.'
distractor_notes.D: The author says such behavior only 'looks irrational' before explaining why it is in fact rational in repeated dealings.

passages.title: Humanities: The Ruin as Art
passages.passage_text:
There is a peculiar pleasure that many viewers take in the sight of a ruin: a roofless abbey, a colonnade broken off mid-arch, a temple whose stones have been half-reclaimed by ivy. The eighteenth-century admirers who first cultivated this taste believed they were responding to something noble in decay itself, as though time were a collaborating artist who improved upon the builder's design. I want to argue that this belief, however charming, mistakes the source of the pleasure and thereby distorts what we owe to the past.

Consider what actually happens when we contemplate a ruin. We do not, in truth, admire the crumbling. We admire the intact structure that the crumbling allows us to imagine. The gap in the wall is pleasing only because we mentally complete the wall; the fractured arch delights because the eye reconstructs its lost curve. The ruin, in other words, is an invitation to an act of imaginative restoration that the visitor performs privately and effortlessly. What the eighteenth century praised as the beauty of decay was really the beauty of a building rebuilt in the mind, purged of every inconvenience that an actual, functioning structure would impose.

This matters because the pleasure is bought at a price the admirer prefers not to name. An intact temple makes demands. It was used for something; it belonged to people whose purposes we may find alien or even repugnant. A ruin, by contrast, has been conveniently emptied of those purposes. We are free to project onto it whatever mood we bring. The sublime melancholy that the connoisseur feels before broken stones is thus a subtle form of possession: the past has been rendered harmless, made available for our contemplation precisely because it can no longer contradict us.

I do not say this pleasure is illegitimate. To imagine is not a crime, and there is genuine feeling in the encounter. But we should not confuse it with reverence. The person who loves a ruin loves an absence he has furnished himself, and calls his own furnishing history. True respect for the past would begin where this pleasure ends, in the harder work of asking what the intact thing meant to those who built it, and being willing to be told an answer we did not wish to hear.

options.B: Eighteenth-century critics were correct that time functions as a collaborating artist that improves upon original designs.
options.C: Ruins should be restored to their original condition so that viewers can appreciate their genuine historical meaning.
options.D: The melancholy inspired by ruins is an illegitimate emotion that thoughtful observers should learn to suppress.

questions.explanation: The author argues that the pleasure of ruins comes not from decay but from an imaginative reconstruction that empties the structure of the original builders' purposes, so the past 'can no longer contradict us' and is 'rendered harmless.' This describes a disguised form of control over an unresisting past, which is the passage's core claim.

distractor_notes.B: This reverses the author's point; he explicitly calls the 'collaborating artist' view a charming mistake that distorts what we owe the past.
distractor_notes.C: The passage never advocates physical restoration; it calls for the intellectual work of asking what the intact thing meant, not for rebuilding.
distractor_notes.D: The author explicitly says the pleasure is not illegitimate ('I do not say this pleasure is illegitimate'), so calling it illegitimate distorts his view.

passages.title: Social Sciences: Trust and Numbers
passages.passage_text:
Why do modern institutions reach so readily for the quantified measure, the ranking, the standardized score? The conventional explanation is that numbers are simply more accurate than judgment, and that a bureaucracy adopts them in a straightforward pursuit of truth. I find this explanation incomplete. Institutions turn to numbers not primarily because numbers are more accurate, but because numbers travel well among strangers who do not trust one another, and this social function is easy to mistake for a purely epistemic one.

Consider the position of an official who must justify a decision to superiors, rivals, and a suspicious public. If she relies on her seasoned judgment, she opens herself to the charge of favoritism, and her expertise, however real, offers no defense the outsider can inspect. A number, by contrast, appears to decide the matter without her. It can be recomputed by anyone; it seems to issue from a procedure rather than a person. The official who says 'the formula ranked this applicant highest' has, in effect, borrowed the authority of an impersonal rule. She is protected not because the number is wiser than she is, but because it is harder to accuse a rule of playing favorites.

This reframing carries a cost that the accuracy story conceals. To make a judgment quantifiable, one must first strip away whatever cannot be counted. A skilled evaluator may know that a particular case is exceptional in ways no column can capture; but the moment she is required to defend herself with a score, that knowledge becomes a liability rather than an asset, because it cannot be shown. Over time, then, the demand for numbers does not merely record expertise; it quietly reshapes what counts as expertise, favoring the measurable over the discerned.

We should therefore resist reading the spread of quantification as a simple triumph of rigor over guesswork. Where mutual trust is strong, professionals are permitted to exercise judgment and are seldom asked to reduce it to figures. It is precisely where trust is thin, where decisions must persuade the skeptical and the distant, that numbers proliferate. The rise of measurement is, in this light, less a sign of how much we have learned than a sign of how little we are willing to take on faith.

questions.stem: The main idea of the passage is that institutions adopt quantified measures primarily because:

options.A: numbers have been proven more accurate than expert judgment in virtually every domain.
options.B: numbers provide a form of impersonal authority that functions where mutual trust is lacking.
options.C: bureaucracies are staffed by officials who are incapable of exercising sound judgment.
options.D: the public has a natural preference for rankings and scores over narrative explanations.

questions.explanation: The author's thesis is that numbers 'travel well among strangers who do not trust one another' and that quantification proliferates 'precisely where trust is thin.' The number lends 'the authority of an impersonal rule' that protects the official. This social/trust function is the central claim.

distractor_notes.A: The author explicitly denies that accuracy is the primary reason and calls the accuracy story 'incomplete.'
distractor_notes.C: The passage assumes officials can have real 'seasoned judgment' and 'expertise'; incapacity is never claimed.
distractor_notes.D: The passage attributes the turn to numbers to institutional needs for defensible authority amid distrust, not to a public taste for rankings.

'Travel well among strangers' thus means they gain acceptance across parties without shared personal trust.

distractor_notes.C: The passage never claims numbers become more accurate as they circulate; that inverts its skepticism about accuracy.
distractor_notes.D: 'Strangers' refers to parties lacking mutual trust, not to people who relocate; this is out of scope.

distractor_notes.D: The reshaping is described as a quiet, gradual effect ('over time'), not a conscious institutional intention to eliminate judgment.

distractor_notes.C: Defending decisions to skeptical parents and journalists is the thin-trust situation that, per the author, invites quantification.
distractor_notes.D: Justifying allocations to auditors and a distrustful committee fits the author's condition of thin trust and distant scrutiny.

questions.stem: Suppose researchers found that two departments with identical levels of internal trust adopted quantified performance metrics at very different rates, driven mainly by whether their industry's regulators demanded audits. How would this finding most likely affect the author's argument?

options.A: It would refute the argument by showing that trust plays no role in the spread of measurement.
options.B: It would be consistent with the argument, since external demands to persuade skeptical outsiders are the kind of thin-trust pressure the author emphasizes.
options.C: It would strengthen the argument by proving that numbers are always more accurate under regulation.

distractor_notes.C: The finding says nothing about accuracy, and the author explicitly downplays accuracy as the driver; this misreads him.
distractor_notes.D: The author's claims about trust generalize beyond a single official to institutions, so a department-level finding is clearly relevant.

passages.title: Humanities: Translating the Untranslatable
passages.passage_text:
It is often said that certain words are untranslatable, that a language may hold a concept for which no other tongue possesses an equivalent, and that the translator faced with such a word must simply surrender. The claim flatters the original language and excuses the translator at once, which may be why it is repeated so fondly. Yet I think it rests on a confusion about what translation is for.

The confusion lies in imagining that a word carries its meaning the way a jar carries water, so that to translate is to pour the same liquid into a differently shaped vessel. On this picture, an untranslatable word is a liquid that will not pour: its meaning is bound to the exact shape of the original. But words do not work this way. A word means what it does because of the countless situations in which speakers have used it, the gestures and occasions and neighboring words that surround each use. Meaning is not a substance sealed inside the word; it is a pattern of use spread across a community's life.

Once we see this, the untranslatable word loses its mystery. What the translator cannot do is find a single foreign word that has lived the same life as the original. But this was never the task. The translator's task is to reproduce, by whatever means the target language allows, the work that the original word did for its readers, and a phrase, a footnote, even a deliberate coinage may accomplish this. To insist on word-for-word equivalence and then lament its absence is to set an impossible standard and call our failure to meet it a property of the words themselves.

There is, I concede, a residue that resists transfer, the particular music of a phrase, the accidents of sound that no rendering preserves. Here the translator truly loses something. But we should not confuse this genuine and narrow loss with the grand claim of untranslatability. To say a poem's music cannot be carried across is modest and true. To say its meaning cannot be is to mistake the difficulty of the craft for an impossibility in the thing, and to grant ourselves a permission we have not earned.

questions.stem: The central argument of the passage is that:

options.A: the claim that words are untranslatable confuses the difficulty of translation with a genuine impossibility.
options.B: translation is ultimately impossible because meaning is a substance sealed inside each word.
options.C: the loss of a phrase's musical qualities proves that no meaning can ever be carried across languages.
options.D: translators should abandon any attempt to render words that lack single-word equivalents in the target language.

questions.explanation: The author argues that meaning is a 'pattern of use' the translator can reproduce by other means, so calling words 'untranslatable' is to 'mistake the difficulty of the craft for an impossibility in the thing.' Distinguishing genuine difficulty from claimed impossibility is his central point.

distractor_notes.B: This is the 'jar carries water' picture the author explicitly rejects; he denies meaning is a sealed substance.
distractor_notes.C: The author restricts genuine loss to musical/sound qualities and denies this shows meaning cannot be carried across.
distractor_notes.D: The author says such words can be rendered by phrases, footnotes, or coinages, the opposite of abandoning the attempt.

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

questions.stem: The author introduces the image of a word carrying meaning 'the way a jar carries water' primarily in order to:

options.A: endorse a widely accepted theory of how words store their meanings.
options.B: present a mistaken model of meaning that the passage then works to dismantle.
options.C: argue that translation is like pouring liquid between differently shaped vessels.
options.D: illustrate the musical qualities of language that resist transfer.

questions.explanation: The jar-and-water image is labeled a 'confusion' that imagines meaning as a substance 'sealed inside the word.' The author immediately counters that 'words do not work this way,' using the image as a flawed model he sets up in order to refute it.

distractor_notes.A: He does not endorse this theory; he calls it a confusion and argues against it.
distractor_notes.C: The pouring analogy is part of the very picture he rejects, not a conclusion he defends.
distractor_notes.D: The jar image concerns meaning as substance, not the musical/sound qualities discussed only in the final paragraph.

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

questions.stem: According to the passage, the author would most likely regard which of the following as the true task of a translator?

options.A: Finding a single target-language word that has been used in exactly the same situations as the original.
options.B: Preserving the precise sounds and rhythm of the original above all other considerations.
options.C: Reproducing, by whatever means the target language allows, the work the original word did for its readers.
options.D: Adding extensive footnotes to every word so that no nuance of the original is ever lost.

questions.explanation: The passage states directly that 'the translator's task is to reproduce, by whatever means the target language allows, the work that the original word did for its readers,' allowing phrases, footnotes, or coinages. This paraphrases that definition.

distractor_notes.A: The author says finding a word that 'has lived the same life as the original' is precisely what the translator cannot and need not do; it 'was never the task.'
distractor_notes.B: Preserving sound is presented as a narrow, genuine loss, not the overriding task; meaning is the priority.
distractor_notes.D: Footnotes are named as one possible means, not a mandate to annotate every word; this overstates his view.

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

questions.stem: The author's tone in describing the claim of untranslatability as something 'repeated so fondly' and granting 'a permission we have not earned' is best characterized as:

options.A: gently skeptical of a comforting idea he believes lets translators off too easily.
options.B: bitterly resentful toward translators he regards as fundamentally dishonest.
options.C: neutral and detached, offering no evaluation of the claim's appeal.
options.D: wholly persuaded that untranslatability is a profound and accurate insight.

questions.explanation: The author notes the claim 'flatters the original language and excuses the translator,' which is why it is 'repeated so fondly,' and says it grants 'a permission we have not earned.' This conveys gentle skepticism toward a comforting idea that excuses translators, without hostility.

distractor_notes.B: 'Bitterly resentful' and 'dishonest' are too extreme; he treats the claim as a fond confusion, not a lie, and even concedes a genuine loss.
distractor_notes.C: He is clearly evaluative, calling the claim a confusion and an unearned permission, not neutral.
distractor_notes.D: He rejects the strong claim of untranslatability, so he is not persuaded it is a profound insight.

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

questions.stem: A critic responds: 'A certain poem's power depends entirely on a pun that exists only in the original language and vanishes in any rendering.' How would this claim most likely be treated within the author's framework?

options.A: As a decisive refutation showing that meaning itself cannot be carried across languages.
options.B: As an instance of the narrow, genuine loss the author concedes, rather than proof of the grand untranslatability claim.
options.C: As irrelevant, because the author denies that any feature of language ever resists transfer.
options.D: As evidence that translators should never attempt to render poetry at all.

questions.explanation: The author explicitly concedes 'a residue that resists transfer, the particular music of a phrase, the accidents of sound.' A pun bound to the original's sound is exactly this narrow, genuine loss, which he distinguishes from the 'grand claim' that meaning cannot be carried across.

distractor_notes.A: The author warns against confusing this narrow loss with the grand claim; he would not treat it as refuting the transferability of meaning generally.
distractor_notes.C: He does not deny all loss; he admits sound and music genuinely resist transfer, so this misstates his view.
distractor_notes.D: He never counsels abandoning translation of poetry; he distinguishes what is lost from what can still be conveyed.

questions.ground_truth_ref: original CARS passage — no external source

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "The restorer who returns a fresco to its original brilliance may believe she honors the artist, yet in scrubbing away the patina of centuries she also erases the painting's biography—the smoke, the damp, the clumsy earlier repairs that are themselves a record of the work's survival. To insist on the 'original' is to choose one moment of a thing's life and pretend it is the whole."

State the author's central claim about art restoration, and explain the tension the author sees between 'the original' and a work's history.

rubric.description: Central claim: restoring a work to its 'original' state erases the historical record it has accumulated.
required_concepts: restoration to the original erases/destroys the work's history/biography
the aging/patina is itself a valuable record
disqualifiers: says the author fully endorses restoration as honoring the artist
says aging has no value

rubric.description: Tension: the 'original' is just one moment of the work's life, wrongly treated as the whole.
required_concepts: the 'original' is only one moment of the object's life
privileging it ignores the rest of the work's existence
disqualifiers: says the author sees no tension
says the whole history equals the original moment

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The author argues that restoring a work to its 'original' brilliance destroys the historical record—the patina, damage, and repairs—that documents its survival. The tension is that the 'original' is merely one moment in the object's long life, which restoration wrongly treats as the work's whole identity.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "We are told that the new translation is 'accessible,' as though the reader were an invalid to be spoon-fed. What is lost when difficulty is smoothed away is precisely the friction that made the original worth the climb."

Describe the author's attitude toward the 'accessible' translation, and quote a word or image from the passage that reveals it.

rubric.description: Attitude: critical/disapproving of the 'accessible' translation; values difficulty.
required_concepts: author is critical/skeptical/disapproving of the accessible translation
values the difficulty of the original
disqualifiers: says the author praises the accessible translation
says the author thinks difficulty is worthless

rubric.description: Cites supporting text (e.g. 'spoon-fed'/'invalid' or 'friction'/'climb').
required_concepts: quotes/points to 'spoon-fed' or 'invalid' or 'friction' or 'worth the climb'
disqualifiers: cites no textual evidence
quotes text unrelated to the author's attitude

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The author is critical and disdainful of the 'accessible' translation, believing it strips away valuable difficulty. Words like 'spoon-fed' and 'invalid' mock the condescension, while 'friction' and 'worth the climb' show the author prizes the challenge of the original.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "Cities that added bike lanes saw cycling rates rise, and planners concluded the lanes had converted drivers into cyclists. But the earliest adopters of bike lanes were cities where cycling was already fashionable; the infrastructure may have followed the demand as much as created it."

Identify the assumption in the planners' conclusion that the author challenges, and explain the alternative causal explanation the author offers.

rubric.description: Assumption challenged: that the bike lanes CAUSED the rise in cycling.
required_concepts: the planners assume the lanes caused/created the increase in cycling
assumes a cause-and-effect direction
disqualifiers: says the author accepts the lanes caused the increase
misidentifies the assumption

rubric.description: Alternative: reverse/confounded causation—existing demand drove lane-building.
required_concepts: pre-existing cycling demand/popularity led cities to build lanes
causation may run the other way / cities self-selected
disqualifiers: offers no alternative explanation
restates the planners' causal claim as the alternative

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The planners assume the bike lanes caused the rise in cycling. The author challenges that causal direction, noting the lanes appeared first in cities where cycling was already popular—so pre-existing demand may have driven lane construction (reverse or confounded causation), not the reverse.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "The historian calls the archive 'innocent,' but no archive is innocent. Someone chose what to keep and what to burn; the silences in the record are as authored as the documents that remain."

Explain what the author means by claiming the archive is not 'innocent,' and what the 'silences' refer to.

rubric.description: 'Not innocent' = the archive is selectively shaped by human choices, not a neutral record.
required_concepts: the archive is selectively created/curated by human choices
not neutral/objective/impartial
disqualifiers: says the archive is a complete, unbiased record
says 'innocent' means factually accurate

rubric.description: 'Silences' = deliberate omissions / what was discarded, which themselves carry meaning.
required_concepts: silences are the omissions / what was destroyed or excluded
these gaps are 'authored' and meaningful
disqualifiers: says silences are meaningless gaps
says silences refer to quiet reading rooms or literal silence

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Calling the archive 'not innocent' means it is not a neutral record but one shaped by deliberate choices about what to preserve and what to destroy. The 'silences' are those omissions—what was burned or left out—which are as intentional and meaningful as the documents that survive.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "The researcher argued that remote work reduces employee productivity, pointing to a drop in measured output at one large firm after it went fully remote."

The claim rests on a single firm. Describe one kind of additional evidence that would most strengthen the general claim, and explain why it would help.

rubric.description: Names apt strengthening evidence: the same effect across many/diverse firms, or a controlled comparison.
required_concepts: evidence from many/diverse firms showing the same drop
a controlled comparison / holding other factors constant
disqualifiers: proposes evidence about only the same single firm
proposes irrelevant evidence

rubric.description: Explains why: broader/controlled data rules out the one firm being unrepresentative or confounded.
required_concepts: broader/controlled data supports generalization
rules out the single firm being a fluke or confounded
disqualifiers: gives no reason
reasoning does not connect the evidence to the claim's weakness

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The claim would be strengthened by evidence that many diverse firms saw the same productivity drop after going remote, ideally with a controlled comparison. That matters because a single firm could be unrepresentative or affected by other changes; broad, controlled data rules out those alternatives and supports generalizing the effect to remote work itself.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "Great cities are not planned; they accrete. The planner's grid is a hypothesis about how people should move, but the desire path worn diagonally across the lawn is the pedestrian's rebuttal—and the pedestrian is always right."

Explain how the image of the 'desire path' functions in the author's argument about how cities develop.

rubric.description: The desire path is evidence/example supporting the claim that cities emerge from actual use.
required_concepts: the desire path is an example/evidence for cities arising from use/accretion
illustrates the central claim
disqualifiers: treats the desire path as merely decorative with no argumentative role
says it supports top-down planning

rubric.description: It functions as the pedestrian's 'rebuttal' to the planner's grid—use overrides design.
required_concepts: it is the pedestrian's rebuttal to the planner's grid
actual use overrides/wins against the planned design
disqualifiers: says the grid overrides the desire path
reverses the author's point

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The desire path is the author's key example: a route worn by actual pedestrian use that contradicts the planner's grid. It functions as concrete evidence for the thesis that cities 'accrete' from real use rather than being dictated by top-down design—the pedestrian's 'rebuttal' shows use overrides the plan.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "A popular theory holds that societies grow more secular as they grow wealthier. Sweden and Japan, prosperous and largely secular, are offered as proof."

Identify the kind of counterexample that would most weaken the wealth-causes-secularization theory, and explain why it undercuts the argument.

rubric.description: Counterexample: a wealthy but highly religious society (or a poor but highly secular one).
required_concepts: a wealthy society that is highly religious
or a poor society that is highly secular
disqualifiers: offers another wealthy secular example (which supports, not weakens)
proposes an irrelevant counterexample

rubric.description: Why it weakens: it breaks the wealth→secularization link, showing wealth isn't sufficient/necessary.
required_concepts: it breaks the claimed correlation between wealth and secularization
shows wealth is not sufficient (or necessary) for secularization
disqualifiers: gives no reason
reasoning does not connect to the causal claim

free_response_questions.reference_answer: A wealthy society that remains highly religious (or a poor society that is highly secular) would most weaken the theory. Such a case breaks the claimed link between wealth and secularization, showing that prosperity is not sufficient to produce secularization and that the two examples given may be coincidental.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "The critic praised the novel for its 'realism,' but realism is a style like any other—a set of conventions we have merely grown used to and stopped noticing. A future age will find our realism as mannered as we find the flourishes of the Baroque."

State a conclusion about the nature of artistic 'realism' that the author would most likely endorse, grounded in the passage.

rubric.description: Conclusion: realism is a set of conventions/a style, not a transparent depiction of reality.
required_concepts: realism is a style/set of conventions
not a neutral or transparent window onto reality
disqualifiers: says the author thinks realism is objectively the truest depiction
says realism is style-free

rubric.description: Grounds it in the passage's logic (conventions we don't notice will later look 'mannered').
required_concepts: because unnoticed conventions will later appear mannered/dated, like the Baroque
realism is historically contingent
disqualifiers: provides no grounding from the passage
grounding contradicts the passage

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The author would endorse the view that 'realism' is not a neutral depiction of reality but simply a style—a set of conventions we no longer notice. The passage grounds this by predicting that a future age will find today's realism as 'mannered' as we find the Baroque, showing realism is historically contingent.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "Standardized tests promise to measure merit while stripping away privilege. Yet the child with a tutor, a quiet room, and three practice attempts arrives at the same exam as the child with none, and we call the gap between their scores 'ability.'"

State the author's central argument about standardized tests, and identify what the author claims the scores actually reflect.

rubric.description: Central argument: the tests do NOT fairly/purely measure merit; they reflect privilege.
required_concepts: standardized tests do not purely/fairly measure merit or ability
they reflect privilege/unequal circumstances
disqualifiers: says the author agrees the tests fairly measure merit
misses the author's critique

rubric.description: Scores reflect unequal resources (tutoring, environment, practice), mislabeled as 'ability.'
required_concepts: scores reflect differences in resources/preparation (tutor, quiet room, practice)
this gap is wrongly called innate 'ability'
disqualifiers: says the score gap is purely innate ability
ignores the resource gap the author stresses

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The author argues that standardized tests fail to deliver on their promise to measure merit free of privilege. What the scores actually reflect is unequal resources—tutoring, a quiet room, repeated practice—which the author says we mislabel as innate 'ability.'

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "Museums speak of 'acquiring' their treasures, a gentle word. It covers purchase and gift, but it also covers the crate pried from a temple wall and shipped across an ocean under armed guard."

Explain the author's purpose in drawing attention to the word 'acquiring,' and describe the author's stance toward museum collections.

rubric.description: Purpose: to show the neutral word 'acquiring' masks/euphemizes coercive taking (looting).

it lumps looting in with purchase and gift
disqualifiers: says 'acquiring' is used to accurately describe fair purchases only
misses the euphemism point

rubric.description: Stance: critical/skeptical of how (some) museum collections were obtained.

disqualifiers: says the author approves of museum acquisition practices
says the author is neutral/indifferent

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The author highlights 'acquiring' to expose it as a euphemism: a gentle word that quietly covers looting—the crate 'pried from a temple wall'—alongside legitimate purchase and gift. The author's stance is critical and skeptical of how museums obtained at least some of their collections.

free_response_questions.prompt:
Passage: "Economists distinguish between risk, where the odds are known, and uncertainty, where they are not. Most consequential decisions—whom to marry, which career to enter—belong to the second category, yet we insist on treating them like the first."

Using the author's risk/uncertainty distinction, explain which category buying insurance against a common, well-documented hazard falls into, and justify your classification.

rubric.description: Correct classification: 'risk' (the odds/probabilities are known).
required_concepts: classifies it as risk
disqualifiers: classifies it as uncertainty
gives no classification

rubric.description: Justification ties to the author's definition (known odds = risk).
required_concepts: a well-documented hazard has known/calculable odds, which the author defines as risk
disqualifiers: justification contradicts the author's definitions
gives no justification
keywords: author defines risk as known odds

free_response_questions.reference_answer: It falls under 'risk.' The author defines risk as decisions where the odds are known and uncertainty as those where they are not. A common, well-documented hazard has calculable probabilities, so insuring against it is a decision made under known odds—risk, not uncertainty.
